name: Platform-neutral fallback authentication framework for Danish digital identity
consequences: Adopting a platform-neutral fallback path shifts trust anchors away from Apple/Google toward standards-based protocols and independent verification. This reduces systemic exposure to platform policy changes, but requires procurement language and regulatory alignment; downstream, it may slow uptake and demand additional certification work, risking short-term continuity gaps.
options: Develop and codify a platform-neutral fallback authentication framework using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect, tested in pilot environments and aligned with eIDAS requirements.
Pilot hardware-token and privacy-preserving browser-based fallbacks that avoid reliance on mobile OS integrity signals while documenting integration with AltID.
Embed fallback paths into procurement requirements with independent attestations of platform trust boundaries and continuous compliance monitoring.
review: Core tension: platform-neutral fallback challenges the established trust anchors and may complicate certification across the MitID ecosystem. The weakness: none of the options fully address the regulatory approval timelines and the risk of interim continuity gaps during migration.

name: Coalition-building and policy engagement for platform-neutral access
consequences: Consolidates political support and frames resilience and procurement neutrality; the direct effect is heightened likelihood of institutional engagement and potentially formal commitments, while downstream risk: resistance from incumbent operators and extended negotiations timelines.
options: Mobilize a formal policy proposal and stakeholder matrix to secure early government engagement and align risk posture across civil society, academia, and policy.
Initiate parliamentary briefings and public consultations to normalize platform-neutral requirements within procurement cycles.
Establish a standing cross-sector coalition with clear decision gates and a plan for escalation when resistance arises.
review: Core tension: coalition-building can generate political momentum but may outpace procurement realism; weakness: the options do not specify concrete decision gates or funding commitments that could anchor momentum.

name: Credible non-production demonstrators to validate fallback access models
consequences: Non-production demonstrators provide evidence of feasibility and interoperability for fallback access models without touching live MitID systems, reducing perceived risk for stakeholders. The direct effect is a credible basis for procurement language and standards pressure, while downstream risk: inflated expectations about near-term deployment and governance complexities.
options: Develop two non-production demonstrators, one mobile-oriented and one browser-based or hardware-token-based, to test standards-based fallback in a simulated identity environment.
Document technical risk, interoperability constraints, and policy implications in a publicly accessible feasibility report.
Engage external security reviews and independent assessments to validate technical plausibility and governance implications.
review: Core tension: demonstrations may imply readiness for production; weakness: the options don’t explicitly address how findings translate into binding procurement requirements or governance actions.

name: Procurement language and vendor risk disclosure to address supplier concentration and continuity risk
consequences: Incorporates explicit risk disclosures and fallback requirements into procurement; direct: reduces single-vendor lock-in and strengthens resilience; downstream: increased procurement complexity and potential pushback from incumbents.
options: Insert explicit supplier-diversity and continuity clauses into AltID procurement requirements to prevent single-vendor lock-in.
Mandate independent security certifications and measurable fallback-readiness metrics for platform-neutral components.
Lobby for formal risk-disclosure requirements and contingency planning in government procurement documentation.
review: Core tension: procurement-focused risk disclosure can provoke resistance from incumbents; weakness: misses how to operationalize cross-border procurement compliance and data-sharing restrictions.

name: EU interoperability and alignment with European Digital Identity Wallet standards
consequences: Aligning with EU requirements broadens interoperability; direct: increases legitimacy and potential access to EU funds and frameworks; downstream: national autonomy trade-offs and slower national adoption if EU measures lag behind national priorities.
options: Engage with EU bodies to influence European Digital Identity Wallet implementation guidance toward platform neutrality and certified fallback capabilities.
Publish a harmonization roadmap detailing Danish position on interoperability, portability, and resilience, integrated into national procurement strategies.
Coordinate with EU working groups to embed procurement-neutrality language into AltID-related design documents.
review: Core tension: EU alignment can boost interoperability but may constrain national tailoring and speed; weakness: options don’t specify how Danish procurement will reconcile EU guidelines with phase-specific milestones.

name: Technical and regulatory governance for independent oversight of identity trust signals
consequences: Establishes independent oversight to maintain accountability and limit vendor dependence; direct: fosters transparency and audits of signals; downstream: adds governance overhead and potential delays in procurement and iteration.
options: Establish an independent governance board to oversee fallback standards, telemetry boundaries, and cross-platform interoperability.
Define risk-sizing methodologies and accountability mechanisms for platform signals to ensure transparent decision-making and auditability.
Institute routine security reviews, third-party testing, and certification cadences to sustain credibility of non-Apple/Google trust anchors.
review: Core tension: governance adds transparency but risks bureaucracy; weakness: lacks a concrete plan for funding and resourcing the oversight body.

name: Open-standards fallback path design for public-sector authentication
consequences: Implementing a standards-based fallback path decouples authentication from device platform signals, reducing single-vendor risk and enabling cross-agency continuity. It requires cross-ministerial standardization and procurement alignment, which can delay rollout but improves long-term resilience.
options: Establish a standards-based fallback path using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect to decouple identity verification from device platform signals.
Pilot phased deployment across three ministries, collecting resilience metrics and privacy observations to inform procurement and governance.
Develop an open-source reference implementation and accompanying API specifications to enable cross-agency integration independent of Apple or Google app ecosystems.
review: Core tension: pursuing platform-neutral standards versus the pace of government procurement; weakness: options do not specify governance mechanisms to coordinate cross-ministry rollout and funding during transition.

name: Independent governance and external security validation integration
consequences: Adds external credibility by validating interoperability and security, and helps public trust; however, it can slow decision cycles and increase oversight costs unless funding and governance interfaces are defined.
options: Form an independent security oversight panel tasked with quarterly reviews of interoperability proofs, risk assessments, and public reporting of test results.
Contract external auditors to perform annual penetration tests and privacy impact assessments on fallback architectures and data flows.
Establish a public dashboard that tracks compliance milestones, vulnerability disclosures, and remediation timelines for all platform-neutral components.
review: Core tension: external validation adds credibility but may create cost and schedule pressure; weakness: options do not specify financing or interaction with existing MitID governance.

name: Strategic EU-aligned regulatory liaison and governance intensification
consequences: Increases influence with EU standards and reduces misalignment risk; can slow national decisions due to multi-party consensus and political considerations.
options: Create a joint Danish-EU task force to map AltID requirements to NSIS, eIDAS, and wallet interoperability, expediting policy alignment.
Secure formal commitments from Danish agencies to pilot EU-aligned fallback standards within phase two.
Draft a policy roadmap that translates EU Digital Identity Wallet guidance into Danish procurement and architecture requirements.
review: Core tension: EU alignment competes with national autonomy and budget cycles; weakness: options lack a plan for potential disagreements within domestic governance.

name: Resilience-focused diversification of identity trust anchors beyond platform ecosystems
consequences: Reduces single-point failure risk by distributing trust across multiple anchors; however, it increases governance overhead and interoperability validation requirements.
options: Establish a multi-anchor architecture with keys stored across government, academia, and trusted industry partners to reduce platform dependence.
Prototype cross-anchor revocation and attestation workflows to ensure rapid retirement of compromised anchors without service interruption.
Develop governance agreements that standardize interoperability testing across anchors and publish results.
review: Core tension: diversification raises operational complexity; weakness: options do not specify regulatory acceptance paths or cross-anchors testing cadence.

name: Procurement and vendor-risk disclosure with performance-contingent incentives
consequences: Improves market transparency and competition while tying resilience outcomes to funding; risk of pushback from incumbents and longer bid cycles.
options: Incorporate mandatory vendor risk disclosures into RFPs, including concentration metrics, dependency graphs, and contingency plans.
Link a portion of contractor payments to resilience performance milestones and verified cross-anchor interoperability tests.
Establish a pre-qualification framework that favors bidders with verifiable fallback-ready capabilities and transparent supply-chain practices.
review: Core tension: market pressure versus incumbents' resistance; weakness: options lack a staged implementation timeline and funding source for incentives.

name: Public-interest privacy, accountability, and transparency framework for fallback access
consequences: Strengthens legitimacy and user trust by embedding privacy protections and accountability; but may slow deployment and limit rapid experimentation.
options: Institute privacy-by-design requirements for fallback components, including data minimization, pseudonymization, and auditable access controls.
Create an independent citizen-review process to assess communications and outreach about platform-neutral access policies and their implications.
Publish regular incident simulations and recovery drills to demonstrate resilience without exposing sensitive operational details.
review: Core tension: privacy protections can delay rollout; weakness: options do not specify metrics for evaluating privacy protections or incident-drill reporting cadence.

name: Fallback authentication architecture integration into AltID procurement framework
consequences: Direct effect: codifies a platform-neutral fallback path as a mandatory criterion in AltID procurement and testing protocols. Downstream implications: raises governance overhead, extends procurement timelines, and requires independent validation of interoperability across devices and browsers.
options: Embed a modular AltID-capable fallback path into the next procurement cycle by requiring open standards compliance and independent testing gates.
Mandate a staged pilot with clearly defined exit criteria for platform-neutral signals in non-production environments across three pilot agencies.
Adopt a risk-sharing contract model with liability for continuity failures and independent verification of fallback interoperability before live rollout.
review: Core tension: embedding a platform-neutral fallback into procurement cycles clashes with existing vendor relationships and internal approval timelines. Weakness: the options miss a concrete budget mechanism and a legislative catalyst to speed adoption and governance alignment.

name: Diversified trust anchors and hardware-token-based validation outside platform duopoly
consequences: Direct effect: broadens trust anchors beyond Apple/Google, reducing platform lock-in. Downstream implications: increases supply-chain complexity, user friction, and training requirements across public-sector users.
options: Adopt a multi-source trust-anchor strategy incorporating hardware tokens and privacy-preserving device attestation.
Pilot browser-based fallback using WebAuthn across multiple vendors and operating systems to reduce platform dependence.
Establish independent third-party certification for cross-vendor interoperability to build procurement confidence.
review: Core tension: expanding trust anchors beyond device OS ecosystems raises supply risk and user accessibility concerns. Weakness: the options do not specify fallback accommodations for users without tokens or incompatible devices.

name: Public-private coalition governance for platform independence and continuity planning
consequences: Direct effect: builds legitimacy and shared risk, reducing single-party capture risk. Downstream implications: potential for governance drift and slower decision pace as more voices contend with priorities.
options: Form a cross-sector coalition to publish a joint resilience roadmap with procurement milestones and risk disclosures.
Create a parliamentary privacy oversight forum to scrutinize fallback design and preserve democratic accountability.
Launch an EU-aligned standards dialogue to harmonize requirements and avoid duplicative vendor lock-in.
review: Core tension: governance coalition can stall progress amid competing agendas. Weakness: no explicit decision rights or budget commitments are articulated in the options.

name: Open standards-based demonstrator program with NSIS-compliant threat modeling
consequences: Direct effect: validates feasibility of fallback approaches through non-production demonstrators and builds credibility for procurement. Downstream implications: may not translate to production readiness and requires sustained funding and governance to scale.
options: Develop non-production demonstrators showcasing FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect across diverse devices to preserve fallback authenticity.
Model NSIS-compliant threat assessments and publish findings to accelerate uptake by procurement and EU standards bodies.
Engage independent security researchers in quarterly reviews to maintain credibility and avoid vendor influence.
review: Core tension: demonstrators validate feasibility but may not guarantee production obligations. Weakness: lacks a formal certification pathway or plan to transition to procurement-stage standards.

name: Regulatory advocacy to embed platform-neutral fallback access in eIDAS2 wallet interoperability requirements
consequences: Direct effect: positions Denmark to influence EU wallet interoperability toward platform neutrality. Downstream implications: risk of pushback from incumbent operators and slow policy cycles as negotiations unfold.
options: Collaborate with national and EU officials to codify fallback authentication requirements within AltID and wallet interoperability specs.
Publish policy notes detailing resilience benefits and risk reductions to justify procurement shifts.
Coordinate with privacy authorities to ensure transparency and protect user consent in fallback signals.
review: Core tension: EU regulatory timelines may outpace national advocacy. Weakness: no explicit sequencing or milestone commitments for EU engagement.

name: Platform-agnostic continuity planning and procurement risk mapping across the AltID program
consequences: Direct effect: improves readiness for disruptions and clarifies dependencies across the identity stack. Downstream implications: increases internal coordination load and requires ongoing maintenance of risk and procurement maps.
options: Assemble a living continuity plan with trigger-based procurement actions and independent audit checkpoints.
Map supplier concentration and dependency paths, then publish a public risk register to build external pressure.
Run procurement pilots with reserve budgets for alternative signals to test resilience under disruption.
review: Core tension: continuity planning requires sustained funding and governance integration. Weakness: options do not specify a concrete governance mechanism to embed into procurement or risk registers.
